title: Branchial (pharyngeal) arch embryology
body_html:
ArchMusclesSkeletal/cartilageNervesArteries
1st (mandibular arch)Muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, medial and lateral pterygoids)
Anterior belly of the digastric
Mylohyoid
Tensor tympani
Tensor veli palatini
Maxilla
Mandible
Incus
Malleus
Meckel's cartilage		Trigeminal nerve (V2 and V3)Maxillary artery
2nd (hyoid arch)Muscles of facial expression
Stapedius
Stylohyoid
Posterior belly of the digastric		Stapes
Temporal styloid process
Hyoid (lesser horn)
Reichert's cartilage		Facial nerveStapedial and hyoid artery
3rdStylopharyngeusHyoid (greater horn)Glossopharyngeal nerveCommon carotid artery, proximal part of internal carotid artery
4thCricothyroid muscleThyroid cartilage
Epiglottic cartilage		Vagus nerve: superior laryngeal branchRight: proximal part of the right subclavian
Left: aortic arch
6thAll intrinsic muscles of larynx except the cricothyroid muscleCricoid cartilage
Arytenoid cartilage
Corniculate cartilage		Vagus nerve: recurrent laryngeal branchRight: Proximal part of right pulmonary artery
Left: left pulmonary artery and ductus arteriosus

Treacher-Collins syndrome
  • autosomal dominant condition
  • caused by a reduced number of neural crest cells migrating to the first and second pharyngeal arches
  • as would be expected from the above table, this results in a number of craniofacial abnormalities including mandibular and zygomatic hypoplasia. This may result in airway problems
  • other features include ear anomalies, cleft palate, colobomas of the lower lids
